title: Parkinson's Disease: Understanding Early Symptoms
summary: Parkinson's often starts small — a slight tremor, a change in handwriting — long before it's diagnosed. Knowing the early pattern helps families get ahead of it.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Parkinson's Disease: Understanding Early Symptoms | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Parkinson's often starts small — a slight tremor, a change in handwriting — long before it's diagnosed. Knowing the early pattern helps families get ahead
whatYouNeedToKnow: A resting tremor (present at rest, easing with movement) is the classic early sign.
Slowed movement and smaller handwriting are common but easy to miss.
Loss of smell and sleep changes can appear years before motor symptoms.

paragraphs: The most recognized early symptom is a tremor, usually in one hand, that's most noticeable when the hand is at rest rather than in use. Alongside this, movements can become slower and smaller — handwriting shrinking over time, a shuffling walk, or a reduced arm swing on one side.

Less well-known early signs include a diminished sense of smell, acting out dreams during sleep, and constipation — all of which can appear well before the motor symptoms that usually prompt a diagnosis.

nextSteps: Note which symptoms appear and on which side of the body.
See a neurologist, ideally one specializing in movement disorders.
Ask about early physical therapy, which has real benefit even before medication starts.
disclaimer: These are common early patterns, not a diagnostic guide. Tremor and slowed movement have several possible causes — a neurologist's evaluation is the only way to know for sure.
